column1: #separator:Semicolon
#html:false
What is an interrupt?;A mechanism by which a peripheral tells the CPU exactly when something happened
column2: so the CPU stops its current work
column4: and resumes — instead of repeatedly asking.

column1: Polling vs interrupts for waiting on an event?;Polling spins reading a status bit (simple and deterministic but monopolises the CPU and wastes power). Interrupts let the peripheral signal the CPU
column2: which is then free to do work or sleep until the event.

column1: How does an interrupt invert control?;Instead of the CPU repeatedly asking the peripheral (polling)
column2: the peripheral tells the CPU when something happened — trading a little complexity for big gains in efficiency

column1: What are the steps when an interrupt is taken?;Finish/abandon the current instruction
column2: save context (return address + state)
column3: look up the handler address in the vector table
column5: then return-from-interrupt restoring the context so the interrupted code resumes unaware.

column1: What does Cortex-M hardware stack automatically on interrupt entry?;Eight registers (R0-R3
column6: so a plain C function can serve as the ISR with no assembly glue.

column1: What is the interrupt vector table?;An array of function-pointer addresses at a fixed location
column2: one slot per interrupt/exception source. The core indexes it to find the right handler.

column1: What are vector-table slots 0 and 1 on Cortex-M?;Slot 0 holds the initial stack pointer
column2: slot 1 holds the reset handler's address. The rest are exception and interrupt handlers in a chip-defined order.

column1: What is the NVIC
column2: and what do vectored and nested mean?;The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller on Cortex-M. Vectored = the hardware itself selects the right handler from the table (no software dispatch). Nested = a higher-priority interrupt can preempt a lower-priority ISR.

column1: What is interrupt latency?;The delay from the request being asserted to the first instruction of the ISR executing — finishing the current instruction
column2: context save
column3: and vector fetch. On Cortex-M it is a fixed published number (e.g. 12 cycles).

column1: What is tail-chaining?;If a second interrupt is pending when an ISR finishes
column2: the core skips the pop-then-push and goes straight into the next ISR

column1: What is late arrival in interrupt handling?;If a higher-priority interrupt arrives during the context save of a lower-priority one
column2: the core switches to the higher-priority handler without redoing the stacking.

column1: What does preemption / nesting of interrupts mean?;If a high-priority interrupt fires while a low-priority ISR is running
column2: it preempts the low one — the low ISR is itself interrupted
column3: the high ISR runs to completion

column1: On Cortex-M
column2: which priority value is most urgent?;Lower numerical value = higher urgency. Priority 0 is the most urgent. Only the upper bits of the priority field are implemented
column3: so e.g. writing 1 and 2 may give the same effective priority.

column1: Why is a variable shared with an ISR dangerous?;An ISR runs asynchronously and can fire between any two machine instructions of your main code
column2: so a shared variable can be read torn or stale — the source of the nastiest firmware bugs.

column1: What two things must any ISR-shared variable have?;It must be volatile (so the compiler always re-reads it
column2: never caches a stale copy) and be accessed atomically (naturally a single instruction
column3: or protected by a critical section).

column1: What is a torn read?;On an 8/16-bit CPU
column2: reading a 32-bit value takes several instructions. If the ISR fires between them you read a half-updated value. Fixed with a critical section (briefly disable the interrupt around the access).

column1: Why must an ISR be kept short?;It steals the CPU from everything of lower priority. Do the minimum (read the byte
column2: clear the flag
column4: then return — defer real work to the main loop or a task.

column1: Why must an ISR clear the interrupt flag?;If you forget
column2: many peripherals re-assert the request immediately and your ISR fires forever.

column1: What must an ISR never do?;Never block (no busy-waits
column2: no delay()
column3: no waiting on another interrupt it has preempted) and no malloc/printf (slow

column1: Interrupt vs exception (trap/fault)?;An interrupt comes from a peripheral (timer
column2: UART). An exception comes from the core reacting to the instruction stream (divide-by-zero
column4: bus fault). On Cortex-M both use the vector table

column1: What is an NMI?;A non-maskable interrupt — it cannot be disabled
column2: reserved for truly critical events such as a clock failure.

column1: What is the lost-wakeup race with sleep?;If the interrupt fires between the if(!flag) test and the __WFI() sleep instruction
column2: the flag is already set but the CPU still sleeps and may never wake. __WFI wakes on a pending interrupt
column3: so latching the request before sleep prevents it.

column1: What is the seqlock pattern for reading a multi-part value lock-free?;Read the overflow count
column2: then the counter
column3: then the overflow count again. If the two reads of the count differ
column5: so retry — a lock-free alternative to a critical section.
